subject: Physics
q:
A proton and an electron are associated with same de-Broglie wavelength. The ratio of their kinetic energies is:
(Assume h=6.63 $\times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}}=9.0 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$ and $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}}=1836$ times $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}$ )
question:
A proton and an electron are associated with same de-Broglie wavelength. The ratio of their kinetic energies is:
(Assume h=6.63 $\times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}}=9.0 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$ and $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}}=1836$ times $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}$ )
solution:
$\lambda$ is same for both
$P=\frac{h}{\lambda}$ same for both
$\mathrm{P}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mK}}$
Hence,
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{K} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{~m}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}}}=\frac{1}{1836}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement $-1$ and Statement $-2$. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
Statement-1 : A metallic surface is irradiated by a monochromatic light of frequency $v>v_0$ (the threshold frequency). The maximum kinetic energy and the stopping potential are $\mathrm{K}_{\max }$ and $\mathrm{V}_0$ respectively. If the frequency incident on the surface doubled, both the $\mathrm{K}_{\max }$ and $\mathrm{V}_0$ are also doubled.
Statement-2 : The maximum kinetic energy and the stopping potential of photoelectrons emitted from a surface are linearly dependent on the frequency of incident light.

options: Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is not the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is false, Statement-2 is true.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is false.

subject: Physics
q:
An electron of mass $m$ is moving in an electric field $\vec{E} = -2E_0\hat{i}$ ($E_0 =$ constant $> 0$), with an initial velocity $\vec{V} = v_0\hat{i}$ ($v_0 =$ constant $> 0$). If $\lambda_0 = \dfrac{h}{4mv_0}$, its de Broglie wavelength at time $t$ is __________.
($e =$ charge of electron)
question:
An electron of mass $m$ is moving in an electric field $\vec{E} = -2E_0\hat{i}$ ($E_0 =$ constant $> 0$), with an initial velocity $\vec{V} = v_0\hat{i}$ ($v_0 =$ constant $> 0$). If $\lambda_0 = \dfrac{h}{4mv_0}$, its de Broglie wavelength at time $t$ is __________.
($e =$ charge of electron)
solution:
The force experienced by the electron in the electric field is given by $\vec{F} = q\vec{E}$.

Since the charge of an electron is $-e$ and the electric field is $\vec{E} = -2E_0\hat{i}$, the force is:

$\vec{F} = (-e)(-2E_0\hat{i}) = 2eE_0\hat{i}$

The acceleration of the electron is:

$\vec{a} = \dfrac{\vec{F}}{m} = \dfrac{2eE_0}{m}\hat{i}$

Using the first equation of motion, the velocity of the electron at time $t$ is:

$\vec{v}(t) = \vec{V} + \vec{a}t = \left(v_0 + \dfrac{2eE_0}{m}t\right)\hat{i}$

The de Broglie wavelength of the electron at time $t$ is:

$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{m|\vec{v}(t)|} = \dfrac{h}{m\left(v_0 + \dfrac{2eE_0}{m}t\right)}$

We are given that $\lambda_0 = \dfrac{h}{4mv_0}$, which implies $h = 4mv_0\lambda_0$. Substituting this into the expression for $\lambda$:

$\lambda = \dfrac{4mv_0\lambda_0}{m\left(v_0 + \dfrac{2eE_0}{m}t\right)}$

Dividing the numerator and the denominator by $mv_0$, we get:

$\lambda = \dfrac{4\lambda_0}{1 + \dfrac{2E_0 e}{m}\dfrac{t}{v_0}}$

Answer: $\dfrac{4\lambda_0}{\left[1 + \dfrac{2E_0 e}{m}\dfrac{t}{v_0}\right]}$

subject: Physics
q:
A photo-emissive substance is illuminated with a radiation of wavelength $\lambda_i$ so that it releases electrons with de-Broglie wavelength $\lambda_e$. The longest wavelength of radiation that can emit photoelectron is $\lambda_0$. Expression for de-Broglie wavelength is given by :
( $\mathrm{m}:$ mass of the electron, $\mathrm{h}:$ Planck's constant and $c$ : speed of light)
question:
A photo-emissive substance is illuminated with a radiation of wavelength $\lambda_i$ so that it releases electrons with de-Broglie wavelength $\lambda_e$. The longest wavelength of radiation that can emit photoelectron is $\lambda_0$. Expression for de-Broglie wavelength is given by :
( $\mathrm{m}:$ mass of the electron, $\mathrm{h}:$ Planck's constant and $c$ : speed of light)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{K}. \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{W} \\ & \lambda_{\mathrm{e}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mK} \cdot \mathrm{E}}}, \mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{i}}}, \mathrm{W}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_0} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~h}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m} \lambda_{\mathrm{e}}^2}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{i}}}-\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_0} \\ & \lambda_{\mathrm{e}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \mathrm{mc}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{i}}}-\frac{1}{\lambda_0}\right)}}\end{aligned}$

q: If the total energy transferred to a surface in time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>48</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>, then the magnitude of the total momentum delivered to this surface for complete absorption will be :

solution:
As we know,

p=Ec, where c is velocity of light.

Therefore, p=6.48×1053×108=2.16×10-3 kg m s-1

What is the number of excess electrons that the oil drop will possess? <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mtext>consider</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>81</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>.
question: An oil drop of the radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> with a density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> is held stationary under a constant electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>55</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the Millikan's oil drop experiment. What is the number of excess electrons that the oil drop will possess? <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mtext>consider</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>81</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>.
solution:
qE=Mg⇒neE=ρ43πr3×g
⇒n×1.6×10-19×3.55×105=3×103×43×π×2×10-33×9.81
⇒n=173×10(3-9-5+19)⇒n=1.73×1010.

subject: Physics
q:
The equation λ=1.227x nm can be used to find the de-Broglie wavelength of an electron. In this equation x stands for :
Where,
m= mass of electron
P= momentum of electron
K= Kinetic energy of electron
V= Accelerating potential in volts for electron
question:
The equation λ=1.227x nm can be used to find the de-Broglie wavelength of an electron. In this equation x stands for :
Where,
m= mass of electron
P= momentum of electron
K= Kinetic energy of electron
V= Accelerating potential in volts for electron
solution:
The de-Broglie's wavelength is given by λ=hmv, where, h is Planck's constant and v is velocity.

Using relation between momentum and kinetic energy, we have λ=h2 mKE

Here, kinetic energy of electron is stated as KE=qV.

Then, we have λ=h2mqV

Putting the values of m=9.1×10-31 kg and q=1.62×10-19 C of electron

We get, λ=1.22V nm.

So, x stand for V.

subject: Physics
q:
The difference between threshold wavelengths for two metal surfaces A and B having work function ϕA=9eV and ϕB=4.5eV in nm is:

{Given, hc=1242 eV nm}
question:
The difference between threshold wavelengths for two metal surfaces A and B having work function ϕA=9eV and ϕB=4.5eV in nm is:

{Given, hc=1242 eV nm}
solution:
The work function is given by 

hcλ=ϕ

⇒λ=hcϕ

By the given data,

λA=12429 nm, λB=12424.5 nm, 

The difference between the wavelengths,

λB-λA=12424.5-12429 nm=12429 nm

=138 nm

q: Consider two separate ideal gases of electrons and protons having same number of particles. The temperature of both the gases are same. The ratio of the uncertainty in determining the position of an electron to that of a proton is proportional to:
question: Consider two separate ideal gases of electrons and protons having same number of particles. The temperature of both the gases are same. The ratio of the uncertainty in determining the position of an electron to that of a proton is proportional to:
solution:
K.E.=32kT
⇒p22m=32kT
⇒p=3kT m
From Heisenberg uncertainty principle
∴ ΔxΔp=h4π
Δx=h4πΔp=h4π3kTm
∴ Δx∝1m
∴ ΔxeΔxp=mpme

subject: Physics
q: In a photoelectric effect experiment, the graph of stopping potential V versus reciprocal of wavelength obtained is shown in the figure. As the intensity of incident radiation is increased :
question: In a photoelectric effect experiment, the graph of stopping potential V versus reciprocal of wavelength obtained is shown in the figure. As the intensity of incident radiation is increased :
options: Slope of the straight line get more steep
solution:
Einstein's photoelectric equation is given by,

eVs=hν-Wo, where Wo is the work function.

⇒Vs=hceλ-Woe, where c=νλ.

Intensity does not affect the frequency and work function of a material. Therefore, the graph does not change with increasing intensity.

subject: Physics
q: The surface of a metal is illuminated alternately with photons of energies E1=4 eV and E2=2.5 eV respectively. The ratio of maximum speeds of the photoelectrons emitted in the two cases is 2. The work function of the metal in (eV) is..........
question: The surface of a metal is illuminated alternately with photons of energies E1=4 eV and E2=2.5 eV respectively. The ratio of maximum speeds of the photoelectrons emitted in the two cases is 2. The work function of the metal in (eV) is..........
solution:
Given,

Energy of two photons are E1=4 eV and E2=2.5 eV

The ratio of maximum speeds of the photoelectrons emitted in the two cases is v1v2=2

Using Einstein equation of photoelectric effect,

KEmax=12mv2=E-ϕ  ...(1)

Where, ϕ is the work function of metal and E is the energy of photon

Now using equation for both the cases we get,

 12mv12=4-ϕ  …(2)

12mv22=2.5-ϕ  ...(3)

Dividing equation (2) and (3) and substitute given values, we get,

v12v22=4-ϕ2.5-ϕ=22

⇒3ϕ=6

⇒ϕ=2 eV

subject: Physics
q:
Statement-1 : When ultraviolet light is incident on a photocell, its stopping potential is $V_0$ and the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is $\mathrm{K}_{\max }$. When the ultraviolet light is replaced by $\mathrm{X}-$ rays, both $\mathrm{V}_0$ and $\mathrm{K}_{\max }$ increase.
Statement-2 : Photoelectrons are emitted with speeds ranging from zero to a maximum value because of the range of frequencies present in the incident light. 
Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
question:
Statement-1 : When ultraviolet light is incident on a photocell, its stopping potential is $V_0$ and the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is $\mathrm{K}_{\max }$. When the ultraviolet light is replaced by $\mathrm{X}-$ rays, both $\mathrm{V}_0$ and $\mathrm{K}_{\max }$ increase.
Statement-2 : Photoelectrons are emitted with speeds ranging from zero to a maximum value because of the range of frequencies present in the incident light. 
Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
options: Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is the correct explanation of Statement-1.

Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is not the correct explanation of Statement-
1.
Statement-1 is false, Statement-2 is true.
Statement-1 is true, Statement- 2 is false.
solution:
Since the frequency of ultraviolet light is less than the frequency of $X$-rays, the energy of each incident photon will be more for X-rays
$K.E_{photoelectron} =h v-\varphi$
Stopping potential is to stop the fastest photoelectron
$$
\mathrm{V}_0=
$$
so, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{E}} \mathrm{E}_{\max }$ and $\mathrm{V}_0$ both increases.
But K.E ranges from zero to K.E $E_{\max }$ because of loss of energy due to subsequent collisions before getting ejected and not due to range of frequencies in the incident light.

If the same surface is illuminated by radiation of double the previous wavelength, then the stopping potential becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The threshold wavelength of the metal is:
question: When radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> is incident on a metallic surface, the stopping potential of ejected photoelectrons is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. If the same surface is illuminated by radiation of double the previous wavelength, then the stopping potential becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The threshold wavelength of the metal is:
solution:
VS=hv-ϕ

4.8=hcλ-ϕ  ...(i)

Using above equation (i) -(ii)

3.2=hcλ-hc2λ

3.2=hc2λ   ...(iii) 

λ=hc6.4

Put in equation (ii)

ϕ=1.6

hcλth=1.6

λth=hc1.6

=hc6.4×4=4λ

subject: Physics
q: A light source of wavelength $\lambda$ illuminates a metal surface and electrons are ejected with maximum kinetic energy of 2 eV . If the same surface is illuminated by a light source of wavelength $\frac{\lambda}{2}$, then the maximum kinetic energy of ejected electrons will be (The work function of metal is 1 eV )
question: A light source of wavelength $\lambda$ illuminates a metal surface and electrons are ejected with maximum kinetic energy of 2 eV . If the same surface is illuminated by a light source of wavelength $\frac{\lambda}{2}$, then the maximum kinetic energy of ejected electrons will be (The work function of metal is 1 eV )
solution:
Einstein's photoelectric equation
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{KE}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\phi_0 \\
& 2 \mathrm{eV}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-1 \mathrm{eV} \\
& \frac{h c}{\lambda}=3 \mathrm{eV} \\
& \mathrm{KE}^{\prime}=\frac{h c}{(\lambda / 2)}-\phi_0=6 \mathrm{eV}-1 \mathrm{eV}
\end{aligned}$
$=5 \mathrm{eV}$

subject: Physics
q: A light wave described by $E=60\left[\sin \left(3 \times 10^{15}\right) t+\sin \left(12 \times 10^{15}\right) t\right]$ (in SI units) falls on a metal surface of work function 2.8 eV. The maximum kinetic energy of ejected photoelectron is (approximately) $\_\_\_\_$ eV. $\left(h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}\right.$. s. and $\left.e=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)$
question: A light wave described by $E=60\left[\sin \left(3 \times 10^{15}\right) t+\sin \left(12 \times 10^{15}\right) t\right]$ (in SI units) falls on a metal surface of work function 2.8 eV. The maximum kinetic energy of ejected photoelectron is (approximately) $\_\_\_\_$ eV. $\left(h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}\right.$. s. and $\left.e=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)$
solution:
Light contains two frequencies: $\omega_1 = 3 \times 10^{15}$ rad/s and $\omega_2 = 12 \times 10^{15}$ rad/s
For maximum KE, use higher frequency $\omega_2$.
Energy: $E_2 = \frac{h\omega_2}{2\pi} = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 12 \times 10^{15}}{2\pi}$
$= \frac{79.2 \times 10^{-19}}{6.28} = 12.6 \times 10^{-19}$ J $= 7.9$ eV
$KE_{max} = E_2 - \phi = 7.9 - 2.8 = 5.1$ eV

subject: Physics
q: Radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> is incident on a photocell. The fastest emitted photoelectron has a speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></math>. If the wavelength is changed to <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, the speed of the fastest emitted photoelectron will be
question: Radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> is incident on a photocell. The fastest emitted photoelectron has a speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></math>. If the wavelength is changed to <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, the speed of the fastest emitted photoelectron will be
solution:
12mv2=hcλ-ϕ ...(i)

12mv'2=43hcλ-ϕ  ...(ii)

From Eqn. (i)

hcλ=12mv2+ϕ

On putting this Equation (ii)

12mv′2=4312mv2+ϕ-ϕ

v'2=43v2+2ϕ3m

⇒v'>43v

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>M</mi></math> at rest disintegrates into two particles of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>M</mi></math>, respectively, having non zero velocities. The ratio of de-Broglie wavelength of particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> to that of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>M</mi></math> will be:
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>M</mi></math> at rest disintegrates into two particles of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>M</mi></math>, respectively, having non zero velocities. The ratio of de-Broglie wavelength of particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> to that of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>M</mi></math> will be:
solution:
λ=hp

Both the particles will move with momentum same in magnitude and opposite in direction.

So de Broglie wavelength of both will be same, i.e., ratio 1:1.

subject: Physics
q:
From the photoelectric effect experiment, following observations are made. Identify which of these are correct
A. The stopping potential depends only on the work function of the metal.
B. The saturation current increases as the intensity of incident light increases.
C. The maximum kinetic energy of a photo electron depends on the intensity of the incident light.
D. Photoelectric effect can be explained using wave theory of light.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
From the photoelectric effect experiment, following observations are made. Identify which of these are correct
A. The stopping potential depends only on the work function of the metal.
B. The saturation current increases as the intensity of incident light increases.
C. The maximum kinetic energy of a photo electron depends on the intensity of the incident light.
D. Photoelectric effect can be explained using wave theory of light.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
(A) Stopping potential depends on both frequency of light and work function.
(B) Saturation current ∝ intensity of light.
(C) Maximum KE depends on frequency.
(D) Photoelectric effect is explained using particle theory. In this phenomenon, when electromagnetic radiation (such as light) hits the material, the emission of electrons takes place. In the photoelectric effect, if the frequency is too low, no electron is seen getting freed. But, if the frequency is high enough, some electrons can be observed. These observations prove that the Light is made of particles. The energy of the particle increases with the frequency.

subject: Physics
q:
Number of photons of equal energy emitted per second by a 6 mW laser source operating at 663 nm is $\_\_\_\_$.
(Given : $\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}$ and $\mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$)
question:
Number of photons of equal energy emitted per second by a 6 mW laser source operating at 663 nm is $\_\_\_\_$.
(Given : $\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}$ and $\mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$)
solution:
Power of laser: $P = 6$ mW $= 6 \times 10^{-3}$ J/s
Energy of a single photon at wavelength $\lambda = 663$ nm:
$E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{663 \times 10^{-9}}$
$E = \frac{6.63 \times 3}{663} \times 10^{-34+8+9} = \frac{19.89}{663} \times 10^{-17} = 3 \times 10^{-19}$ J
Number of photons emitted per second:
$N = \frac{\text{Total Energy per second}}{\text{Energy per photon}} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-3}}{3 \times 10^{-19}} = 2 \times 10^{16}$

q:
The ratio of the de-Broglie wavelengths of proton and electron having same kinetic energy:
(Assume mp=me×1849)
question:
The ratio of the de-Broglie wavelengths of proton and electron having same kinetic energy:
(Assume mp=me×1849)
solution:
The de-Broglie wavelength is given by 

λ=hp=h2mK (As, p2=2mK).

It is given that

 (K)e=(K)p

Thus, the ratio of the wavelengths of the proton and electron is 
       
      λpλe=2Kme2Kmp=memp=11849=143.

\end{aligned}$
So blue light option (B)

q: UV light of $4.13 \mathrm{eV}$ is incident on a photosensitive metal surface having work function $3.13 \mathrm{eV}$. The maximum kinetic energy of ejected photoelectrons will be:
question: UV light of $4.13 \mathrm{eV}$ is incident on a photosensitive metal surface having work function $3.13 \mathrm{eV}$. The maximum kinetic energy of ejected photoelectrons will be:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}_{\text {photon }}=(\text { work function })+\mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\max } \\ & \therefore 4.13=3.13+\mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\max } \\ & \therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{max}}=1 \mathrm{eV}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: For a certain metal, when monochromatic light of wavelength $\lambda$ is incident, the stopping potential for photoelectrons is $3V_0$. When the same metal is illuminated by light of wavelength $2\lambda$, then the stopping potential becomes $V_0$. The threshold wavelength for photoelectric emission for the given metal is $\alpha\lambda$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
question: For a certain metal, when monochromatic light of wavelength $\lambda$ is incident, the stopping potential for photoelectrons is $3V_0$. When the same metal is illuminated by light of wavelength $2\lambda$, then the stopping potential becomes $V_0$. The threshold wavelength for photoelectric emission for the given metal is $\alpha\lambda$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$eV_s = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

For the first case:

$3eV_0 = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

For the second case:

$eV_0 = \dfrac{hc}{2\lambda} - \Phi$

Multiplying the second equation by $3$ and equating with the first equation:

$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi = \dfrac{3hc}{2\lambda} - 3\Phi$

$2\Phi = \dfrac{3hc}{2\lambda} - \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$

$2\Phi = \dfrac{hc}{2\lambda}$

$\Phi = \dfrac{hc}{4\lambda}$

Since the work function $\Phi = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_0}$, where $\lambda_0$ is the threshold wavelength:

$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda_0} = \dfrac{hc}{4\lambda}$

$\lambda_0 = 4\lambda$

Given $\lambda_0 = \alpha\lambda$, we get $\alpha = 4$.

Answer: $4$

subject: Physics
q: A photoelectric surface is illuminated successively by monochromatic light of wavelengths <math><mi>λ</mi><mi> </mi><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d </mi><mfrac><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> If the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons in the second case is 3 times that in the first case, the work function of the surface is :
question: A photoelectric surface is illuminated successively by monochromatic light of wavelengths <math><mi>λ</mi><mi> </mi><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d </mi><mfrac><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> If the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons in the second case is 3 times that in the first case, the work function of the surface is :
solution:
K.Eλ=hcλ-ϕ

K.Eλ2=hcλ2-ϕ

K.Eλ2=3K.Eλ

⇒  ϕ=hc2λ

subject: Physics
q:
In a photoelectric experiment, the wavelength of the light incident on a metal is changed from $300 \mathrm{nm}$ to $400 \mathrm{nm}$. The decrease in the stopping potential is close to :

$\left(\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\mathrm{e}}=1240 \mathrm{nm}-\mathrm{V}\right)$
question:
In a photoelectric experiment, the wavelength of the light incident on a metal is changed from $300 \mathrm{nm}$ to $400 \mathrm{nm}$. The decrease in the stopping potential is close to :

$\left(\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\mathrm{e}}=1240 \mathrm{nm}-\mathrm{V}\right)$
solution:
Let $\phi=$ work function of the metal,

$\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{1}}=\phi+\mathrm{eV}_{1}$

$\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{2}}=\phi+\mathrm{eV}_{2} \quad \ldots \ldots$

(ii)

Sutracting (ii) from (i) we get

$\mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}\right)=\mathrm{e}\left(\mathrm{V}_{1}-\mathrm{V}_{2}\right)$

$\Rightarrow V_{1}-V_{2}=\frac{h c}{e}\left(\frac{\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \cdot \lambda_{2}}\right)\left[\begin{array}{l}\lambda_{1}=300 \mathrm{nm} \\ \lambda_{2}=400 \mathrm{nm} \\ \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\mathrm{e}}=1240 \mathrm{nm}-\mathrm{V}\end{array}\right]$

$=(1240 \mathrm{nm}-\mathrm{v})\left(\frac{100 \mathrm{nm}}{300 \mathrm{nm} \times 400 \mathrm{nm}}\right)$

$=1,03 \mathrm{~V} \approx 1 \mathrm{~V}$
